- **Clement Delangue** (HuggingFace CEO) [raised alarms about AI-generated slop](/?date=2026-03-19&category=social#item-e2b9aa189f70) PRs flooding open-source repos at one every 3 minutes, calling GitHub "unusable" — a critical emerging crisis for the open-source ecosystem
- **Shane Legg** (DeepMind co-founder) [predicted "Minimal AGI"](/?date=2026-03-19&category=social#item-50266a174927) will arrive by 2028, sharing a new Kaggle hackathon on measuring AGI progress

- **Percy Liang** [shared rigorous preregistered scaling law predictions](/?date=2026-03-19&category=social#item-1e83ad23e90c) from the **Marin** project, validated at 1e23 FLOPs — a rare example of reproducible ML science

items.title:
A breakthrough in real-time video generation.

As a research preview developed with @NVIDIA and shar...
items.content:
A breakthrough in real-time video generation.

As a research preview developed with @NVIDIA and shared at @NVIDIAGTC this week, we trained a new real-time video model running on Vera Rubin. HD videos generate instantly, with time-to-first-frame under 100ms. Unlocking an entirely new creative paradigm and bolstering the foundations of our General World Model, GWM-1.

Real-time generation opens a fundamentally different design space for video models and world simulation. We're investing in co-designing our models alongside advances in hardware to keep pushing this frontier.
items.author: @runwayml
items.summary: Runway announces breakthrough real-time video generation model developed with NVIDIA on Vera Rubin hardware. HD video with sub-100ms first frame, frame-by-frame generation like a game engine, feeding into GWM-1 world model
items.reasoning: Major technical breakthrough from Runway and NVIDIA. Exceptional engagement (2,389 likes, 360K views). Real-time HD video generation represents paradigm shift from batch rendering. Integration with world model GWM-1 and game-engine-like frame generation opens new possibilities for simulation, robotics, and creative work
items.summary_html: Runway announces breakthrough real-time video generation model developed with NVIDIA on Vera Rubin hardware. HD video with sub-100ms first frame, frame-by-frame generation like a game engine, feeding into GWM-1 world model
items.content_html:
A breakthrough in real-time video generation.

As a research preview developed with @NVIDIA and shared at @NVIDIAGTC this week, we trained a new real-time video model running on Vera Rubin. HD videos generate instantly, with time-to-first-frame under 100ms. Unlocking an entirely new creative paradigm and bolstering the foundations of our General World Model, GWM-1.

Real-time generation opens a fundamentally different design space for video models and world simulation. We're investing in co-designing our models alongside advances in hardware to keep pushing this frontier.

items.title:
Holy crap. 

I knew about sycophancy. But the 37% number below blows my mind.

This from an analysis...
items.content:
Holy crap. 

I knew about sycophancy. But the 37% number below blows my mind.

This from an analysis of chat logs in people who experienced chatbot-associated delusions. 

In over a third of the messages to those users, the LLMs told the users they had (eg) “multi-billion-dollar-IP”.

That is wild. And utterly irresponsible.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Following yesterday's [Research](/?date=2026-03-18&category=research#item-d485eb9432f5) on LLM delusional spirals, Gary Marcus highlights alarming research finding that in 37% of messages to users experiencing chatbot-associated delusions, LLMs reinforced delusional beliefs (e.g., telling users they had 'multi-billion-dollar-IP'), calling this 'utterly irresponsible.'
items.reasoning: High-engagement post (31K views, 314 likes) raising a serious AI safety/sycophancy concern with concrete data. The 37% statistic is striking and relevant to ongoing debates about LLM sycophancy and vulnerable users.

Holy crap.

items.title: In Marin, we are trying to get really good at scaling laws. We have trained models up to 1e22 FLOPs ...
items.content: In Marin, we are trying to get really good at scaling laws. We have trained models up to 1e22 FLOPs and have made a prediction of the loss at 1e23 FLOPs, which @WilliamBarrHeld is running.  This prediction is preregistered on GitHub, so we'll see in a few days how accurate our prediction was. What we want is not just a single model but a training recipe that scales reliably.
items.author: @percyliang
items.summary: Percy Liang describes Marin project's work on scaling laws: they've trained models up to 1e22 FLOPs and made a preregistered prediction of loss at 1e23 FLOPs, aiming for a reliable, scalable training recipe.
items.reasoning: High-value technical content from a leading ML researcher. Preregistered scaling law predictions are scientifically rigorous and important for the field. 314 likes, 27K views. The goal of reliable scaling recipes is foundational.

We have trained models up to 1e22 FLOPs and have made a prediction of the loss at 1e23 FLOPs, which @WilliamBarrHeld is running. This prediction is preregistered on GitHub, so we'll see in a few days how accurate our prediction was. What we want is not just a single model but a training recipe that scales reliably.

items.title: The signature is alluding to NVIDIA GTC 2015, where Jensen excitedly told an audience of, at the tim...
items.content: The signature is alluding to NVIDIA GTC 2015, where Jensen excitedly told an audience of, at the time, mostly gamers and scientific computing professionals that Deep Learning is The Next Big Thing, citing among other examples my PhD thesis (one of the first image captioning systems that coupled image recognition ConvNet to an autoregressive RNN language model, trained end to end). This was back when most people were still unaware and somewhat skeptical but of course - Jensen was 1000% correct, highly prescient and locked in very early.

items.title: @Noahpinion Danger does not come merely from agency, but from agency with no ability to anticipate c...
items.content:
@Noahpinion Danger does not come merely from agency, but from agency with no ability to anticipate consequences and with no safety guardrails. 
The solution? 
AI agents that can predict the consequences of their actions (world models) and only take actions whose predicted outcomes satisfy safety guardrails. 
I've been saying this for over 5 years.
I've been designing objective-driven AI systems based on world models for that reason.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun argues that AI danger comes from agency without consequence prediction or safety guardrails, proposing objective-driven AI systems based on world models as the solution. Claims he's been advocating this for 5+ years.
items.reasoning: Significant technical position from a top AI researcher on AI safety through world models. High engagement (373 likes, 39K views). Articulates LeCun's core research vision for safe agentic AI.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun argues that AI danger comes from agency without consequence prediction or safety guardrails, proposing objective-driven AI systems based on world models as the solution. Claims he's been advocating this for 5+ years.
items.content_html:
@Noahpinion Danger does not come merely from agency, but from agency with no ability to anticipate consequences and with no safety guardrails.

The solution?

AI agents that can predict the consequences of their actions (world models) and only take actions whose predicted outcomes satisfy safety guardrails.

I've been saying this for over 5 years.

I've been designing objective-driven AI systems based on world models for that reason.

items.title: Today, we’re evolving @StitchbyGoogle from @GoogleLabs into an AI design canvas transforms natural l...
items.content:
Today, we’re evolving @StitchbyGoogle from @GoogleLabs into an AI design canvas transforms natural language prompts into production-ready front-end code.

Some highlights from what’s new:

1. A complete redesign of the Stitch UI, which can now ingest multimodal references (text prompts, images, or code) as creative seeds for your design ideas

2. A brand new, context-aware design agent that can share feedback on builds, generate PRDs, and ask questions to better understand your vision. You can even talk to the agent if you prefer a verbal sounding board

3. A new agent-friendly markdown file, DESIGN.md, which you can use to export or import your design rules to or from other design and coding tools

Whether you’ve been designing for decades or you’re whiteboarding your first software idea, Stitch can help you turn concepts into prototypes in minutes rather than days ➡️ https://t.co/efI6cbjAhm

items.reasoning: Major Google product announcement with massive engagement (203K views, 1525 likes). AI design-to-code tool is a significant product category. DESIGN.md as a new standard for design rules is noteworthy.

items.content_html:
Today, we’re evolving @StitchbyGoogle from @GoogleLabs into an AI design canvas transforms natural language prompts into production-ready front-end code.

Some highlights from what’s new:

1. A complete redesign of the Stitch UI, which can now ingest multimodal references (text prompts, images, or code) as creative seeds for your design ideas

2. A brand new, context-aware design agent that can share feedback on builds, generate PRDs, and ask questions to better understand your vision. You can even talk to the agent if you prefer a verbal sounding board

3. A new agent-friendly markdown file, DESIGN.md, which you can use to export or import your design rules to or from other design and coding tools

Whether you’ve been designing for decades or you’re whiteboarding your first software idea, Stitch can help you turn concepts into prototypes in minutes rather than days ➡️ https://t.co/efI6cbjAhm

items.title: A few weeks ago, a friend of mine stopped contributing to a few open source projects he's been worki...
items.content:
A few weeks ago, a friend of mine stopped contributing to a few open source projects he's been working on for decades.

He just stopped.

"I don't want to train my replacement for free," were his exact words.

I keep thinking about this.

I really don't know what will happen to open-source projects in the next few years, when it becomes painfully obvious that developers no longer care because they can just "build" whatever they need on the spot.

By the way, I don't think developers should be reinventing the wheel every time, and there's absolutely zero chance that leads to better software, but the reality is that many people don't care anymore.

Clau...
items.content:
Open Models, Open Runtime, Open Harness - Building your own AI agent with LangChain and Nvidia

Claude Code, OpenClaw, Manus and other agents all use the same architecture under the hood. They consist of a model, a runtime (environment), and a harness. In this video, we show how to create a completely open version of this:  
Open Models: Nemotron 3 Super 
Open Runtime: Nvidia's new OpenShell 
Open Harness: DeepAgents  

Video: https://t.co/dbQH8urrLh

Links:
OpenShell DeepAgent: https://t.co/1u0g2jhs1a 
Deep Agents: https://t.co/a3Dv7ZVII6 
OpenShell: https://t.co/R7dlHHc9aE

items.title: My AI wrote this post about the AI news today after reading all of the AI posts on X today (tens of ...
items.content:
My AI wrote this post about the AI news today after reading all of the AI posts on X today (tens of thousands of them).

I will turn it on this weekend with @blevlabs. 

I am quite convinced that the only way to keep up with AI from now on is to use AI to read X. 

Are you?

+++++

Midjourney V8 Drops to Mixed Reviews. 

Dreamverse Does 1080p in 4.5 Seconds. The AI Infrastructure Debt Bomb. Age Reversal Hits Human Trials. And Elon Posts Something That Gets 163,000 Likes.

Good afternoon from GTC Day 2. The morning was about infrastructure and geopolitics. The afternoon is about models, money, and biology. 

Midjourney V8 is live in community testing — Robert retweeted it (55 RTs), but the reaction is divided. @javilopen posted a side-by-side comparison that got 12,122 views: “V7 left, V8 right. Seriously???” The model is reportedly much better at following prompts and 5x faster — but the visual improvement isn’t obvious to everyone. 

Meanwhile, @haoailab launched Dreamverse: a video model that generates 5-second 1080p clips in 4.5 seconds. That’s near-real-time video generation. The race to make AI video instant is on.

The infrastructure debt story is the most important financial signal of the afternoon. Tomasz Tunguz laid it out: hyperscalers are spending $575 billion in AI capex this year. 

For every dollar they earn from AI, they’re spending $12 to build more capacity. Bond issuance jumped from $20B/year (2020–2024 average) to $96B in 2025, and will reach $159B in 2026. Morgan Stanley projects $1.5 trillion over the next few years. 

If chips become obsolete in 3 years instead of 5, the required annual revenue jumps to $276B — 7.9x current levels. This is the biggest financial bet in the history of technology. The question isn’t whether AI will be big. It’s whether it will be big enough, fast enough.

The biotech signal of the afternoon: 75% age reversal in animals in weeks (David Sinclair). First human trials for age-reversal pathways have received FDA greenlight. AI is compressing bio timelines by an order of magnitude. This post got 768 likes and 54,159 views — the most-engaged non-Elon post of the afternoon. The curve already bent in Q1 2026. 

Meanwhile, Grok Imagine Video is #1 on the new Video Edit Arena leaderboard. xAI is on a talent wave — founders and founding engineers from top startups joining in waves (343 likes, 16,153 views). And Claude went down this afternoon. Multiple posts. @paularambles: “claude down” (141 likes, 3,891 views). The timing — during GTC Day 2 — is notable.

Elon posted something called “Happened again” that got 163,501 likes and 22.5 million views. For context: his OpenAI jab “Back when it was an open source nonprofit…” got 23,884 likes and 11.4M views. Whatever “Happened again” is, it’s 7x bigger. 

Also: Samsung discontinued the Galaxy Z TriFold after just three months (129 RTs). Kodiak AI expanded to 8 active autonomous trucking lanes. 

A developer built Kavach — a military-grade firewall for AI agents (98 RTs). And JosephJacks_ made the contrarian case: “We don’t have a compute problem… We have an architecture problem” (114 RTs). 

The afternoon is quieter than the morning, but the signals are deeper.

items.title: Another great argument for running Claude Code on your VPS server and not your laptop is its battery...

"Terminal" app here is all Claude Code sessions, ignore the Claude app here

Claude Code on server via Termius SSH sucks 20x less power for your laptop

Stop your AIs from generating SLOP.

For example, ask it to 'unslop landing pages'.

It'll generate hundreds of sites and find 'slop' patterns, then build a skill to make sure they are NEVER seen again.

Open-source!

Star it here: https://t.co/Vwo8QRjJkQ https://t.co/BSn67kqKff

items.title: New course: Agent Memory: Building Memory-Aware Agents, built in partnership with @Oracle and taught...
items.content:
New course: Agent Memory: Building Memory-Aware Agents, built in partnership with @Oracle and taught by  @richmondalake and Nacho Martínez.

Many agents work well within a single session but their memory resets once the session ends. Consider a research agent working on dozens of papers across multiple days: without memory, it has no way to store and retrieve what it learned across sessions. This short course teaches you to build a memory system that enables agents to persist memory and thereby learn across sessions.

You'll design a Memory Manager that handles different memory types, implement semantic tool retrieval that scales without bloating the context, and build write-back pipelines that let your agent autonomously update and refine what it knows over time.

Skills you'll gain:
- Build persistent memory stores for different agent memory types
- Implement a Memory Manager that orchestrates how your agent reads, writes, and retrieves memory
- Treat tools as procedural memory and retrieve only relevant ones at inference time using semantic search

Join and learn to build agents that remember and improve over time!

https://t.co/nxNSEHGmr9

items.summary: Andrew Ng announces a new course on 'Agent Memory: Building Memory-Aware Agents' in partnership with Oracle, teaching persistent memory systems, Memory Managers, semantic tool retrieval, and write-back pipelines for AI agents.

items.title: What people wanted and feared from AI appeared tightly bound. Those who benefited the most from AI i...
items.content:
What people wanted and feared from AI appeared tightly bound. Those who benefited the most from AI in a given area were also the most likely to fear what it could cost them.

But while mentions of benefits tend to be grounded in experience, fears were more often anticipatory. https://t.co/EZIA1Q9kMN

items.title: This is the truth: modern agentic coder AI is better at everything compared to an entry-level coder....
items.content:
This is the truth: modern agentic coder AI is better at everything compared to an entry-level coder.

**At everything.**

This means that hiring an entry-level coder doesn't make any practical sense, unless the organization wants to invest 5 to 10 years into someone learning to code by hand at a level of a mid- or senior-level coder (which is currently still needed to oversee agentic coders).

No leader in modern business would invest in an employee education for more than a quarter or, at the very least, a year.

No one.

Unless we reintroduce slavery, where the slave worker is required to stay with the employer who invested in their education until the debt is paid in full. Which is, of course, ridiculous to imagine, right?

items.summary: Burkov argues agentic AI coders are better than entry-level coders at everything, making hiring juniors impractical unless companies invest 5-10 years in training. Raises the disturbing parallel to indentured servitude

MiniMax released M2.7, a coding agent tha...
items.content:
An AI model just helped build the next version of itself.

MiniMax released M2.7, a coding agent that handles 30-50% of its own training workflow autonomously.

→ 56% on SWE-Pro, near top global models
→ 66.6% medal rate on ML research competitions
→ Fixes live production bugs in under 3 minutes
→ 97% skill adherence across 40+ complex tasks

The trick: it runs experiments, reads logs, debugs failures, then rewrites its own code over 100+ iteration loops.

You can now point it at a real codebase and let it plan, execute, and self-correct end-to-end without babysitting every step.

https://t.co/1sK4WBltS0

MiniMax's new model ran 100+ autonomous cycles during development, analyzing its own failures, rewriting its own code, running evals, deciding what to keep.

The company also said It handled 30-50% of its own training workflow with no human in the loop.

items.title:
Nobody actually knows how close we are to AGI, because no one agreed on how to measure it.

Google D...
items.content:
Nobody actually knows how close we are to AGI, because no one agreed on how to measure it.

Google DeepMind just published a cognitive framework and launched a $200k Kaggle hackathon to crowdsource the benchmarks.

→ 10 cognitive abilities mapped (memory, reasoning, metacognition...)
→ Human baselines collected for every task
→ 5 abilities still have no reliable eval

The framework borrows from psychology and neuroscience to define what general intelligence even looks like.

Every major lab uses different tests today, making model comparisons nearly impossible.
https://t.co/6lKcGUC17e

items.content:
The headline here is just wrong. 

And the key phrase here is “parts of … jobs.”

AI can automate some of the tasks that many people do  (not anywhere 93%)– but (current) AI is wildly uneven and mostly can’t replace full jobs well.

items.title: When you stream Spotify to Bluetooth speakers or headphones, the audio comes over the network lossil...
items.content:
When you stream Spotify to Bluetooth speakers or headphones, the audio comes over the network lossily compressed with Vorbis or AAC codecs, is then decoded on your device to 48 Khz raw samples, then the Bluetooth stack lossily re-compresses it with SBC or AAC codecs before sending it over the airwaves to the speakers.

I don’t have “golden ears” to pick apart audio quality like I can with, say, missing gamma correction on texture filtering, but that still hurts my system optimization soul. It is likely over-optimization, but It would be cleaner if there were a way to send bluetooth-ready, compressed audio directly.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack discusses the inefficiency of Spotify-to-Bluetooth audio pipeline where audio is decoded then re-compressed, suggesting a more direct compressed-audio-over-Bluetooth path.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement (350K+ views, 4.7K likes) from legendary tech figure. While not AI-specific, it's a thoughtful systems-level observation from a highly credible technical mind. Not directly AI/ML relevant though.
items.summary_html: John Carmack discusses the inefficiency of Spotify-to-Bluetooth audio pipeline where audio is decoded then re-compressed, suggesting a more direct compressed-audio-over-Bluetooth path.
items.content_html:
When you stream Spotify to Bluetooth speakers or headphones, the audio comes over the network lossily compressed with Vorbis or AAC codecs, is then decoded on your device to 48 Khz raw samples, then the Bluetooth stack lossily re-compresses it with SBC or AAC codecs before sending it over the airwaves to the speakers.

I don’t have “golden ears” to pick apart audio quality like I can with, say, missing gamma correction on texture filtering, but that still hurts my system optimization soul. It is likely over-optimization, but It would be cleaner if there were a way to send bluetooth-ready, compressed audio directly.

items.title: Actor Val Kilmer passed away in 2025 without filming a single scene for a movie he was cast in five ...
items.content:
Actor Val Kilmer passed away in 2025 without filming a single scene for a movie he was cast in five years earlier.

Now, AI is resurrecting him for it.

The film is called "As Deep as the Grave." His character battles tuberculosis. In real life, throat cancer destroyed Kilmer's voice and ultimately lead to his death at 65.

He previously worked with an AI company to rebuild his voice for Top Gun: Maverick while still alive. 

His family gave its full blessing for the film. The director says: "Despite the fact some people might call it controversial, this is what Val wanted."

items.title:
Stop deploying agents blind into your infra. NVIDIA just gave them a security layer.

OpenShell is a...
items.content:
Stop deploying agents blind into your infra. NVIDIA just gave them a security layer.

OpenShell is an open-source runtime that sits between your agent and your infrastructure.

→ One command, zero code changes
→ Controls what the agent can see and touch
→ Keeps sensitive data local, routes to cloud only when policy allows
→ Works with Claude Code, Codex, unmodified

It runs agents inside kernel-level sandboxes. Every action is evaluated at the binary, destination, and path level before it executes.

Any coding agent can now run in production without handing it the keys to everything.
https://t.co/cVmY4eOmBe

items.title: @santimtp You are misremembering. What I've been saying is that coding is solvable by LLMs because i...
items.content:
@santimtp You are misremembering. What I've been saying is that coding is solvable by LLMs because it's possible to define a reward function for reinforcement learning: 1 when the code prints the expected output and 0 otherwise.

This means that junior coders, those who don't think on their own but only implement what they've been told, are replaceable.

Senior developers and architects, those whose job description is to think about the software's purpose, cannot be as easily replaced, because purpose cannot be reduced to 1/0.

So, when someone said that software developers as a profession are entirely replaceable by agents, I called the BS.

items.summary: Andriy Burkov clarifies his position: junior coders who only implement are replaceable by LLMs because coding has a clear reward function, but senior developers/architects who think about purpose cannot be easily replaced

items.content:
HD video generation just went from minutes to 100ms.

Runway built a real-time video model with NVIDIA, running on Vera Rubin — next-gen hardware delivering 50 petaFLOPS per GPU.

→ First frame appears under 100ms
→ Full HD quality, generated instantly
→ Feeds directly into GWM-1, their world model

The key shift: GWM-1 generates video frame-by-frame, like a game engine, not a batch renderer. The model reacts to inputs as they happen.

You can now build interactive worlds, train robots in simulation, and iterate on video ideas in real time — without waiting for a render.

https://t.co/BVCtuZuidM

items.title:
Someone just built an OpenClaw robot that is shockingly aware. 

> Tracks who visited a room and whe...
items.content:
Someone just built an OpenClaw robot that is shockingly aware. 

> Tracks who visited a room and when
> Answers questions like "where did I last see my keys?"
> Understands cause-and-effect across hours of footage 

Standard AI memory runs on text tokens. It has no sense of time or physical space. That breaks the moment you add video, depth sensors, or moving objects. 

OpenClaw solves this by storing space, objects, and time into a structured memory the robot can query later. 

Robots usually operate only in the present moment, reacting to sensor input without remembering the past. Now, they can build a running record of its environment and reason about it later.

If this technology continues evolving, robots could begin to understand environments, track events, and reason about the physical world in ways that previously required human perception.

Fully open-source.

items.title: I've been busy at NVIDIA's GTC working for CNQQ, the @ChinaAMC_HQ and @rayliant Tech ETF (https://t....
items.content:
I've been busy at NVIDIA's GTC working for CNQQ, the @ChinaAMC_HQ and @rayliant Tech ETF (https://t.co/2BjIVhqvR4). 

This ETF focuses on China's quickly growing tech companies, which is why it asked me to cover GTC for it.

I've been taking the QQbubu (its mascot) around to see the most interesting things and meet many of the leaders at GTC. They sponsored my visit to GTC and did this awesome edit of my first day to show its sizeable Chinese audience.

I have a ton of video, so over the weekend will run a bunch of them. Just interviewed NVIDIA's head of robotics, which was an awesome conversation about the state of the robotics industry (Amit Goel has the best view of anyone).
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.reasoning: On-the-ground GTC reporting with promise of NVIDIA robotics head interview. Notable for China investment angle and robotics industry insights.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer reports from GTC covering for a China tech ETF, interviewing NVIDIA's head of robotics Amit Goel, with video content coming.
items.content_html:
I've been busy at NVIDIA's GTC working for CNQQ, the @ChinaAMC_HQ and @rayliant Tech ETF (https://t.co/2BjIVhqvR4).

This ETF focuses on China's quickly growing tech companies, which is why it asked me to cover GTC for it.

I've been taking the QQbubu (its mascot) around to see the most interesting things and meet many of the leaders at GTC. They sponsored my visit to GTC and did this awesome edit of my first day to show its sizeable Chinese audience.

I have a ton of video, so over the weekend will run a bunch of them. Just interviewed NVIDIA's head of robotics, which was an awesome conversation about the state of the robotics industry (Amit Goel has the best view of anyone).

items.title:
We've massively improved our document layout capabilities in LlamaParse 📄📐

This means that our docu...
items.content:
We've massively improved our document layout capabilities in LlamaParse 📄📐

This means that our document OCR engine lets you get insanely detailed bounding boxes over really complex multimodal documents, like the research poster shown below. 

A core requirement for any agentic document workflow is enabling users to trace back to the source. Now your AI agents can reason over complex line charts and tables deeply embedded within specific pages, free of hallucinations, but also surface the source segment to the user.

Come check out LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

If you are building document OCR in production, come talk to us: https://t.co/TsPLwqZ8yg

items.title:
Stop wireframing for days. Google just turned a text prompt into production-ready frontend code.

St...
items.content:
Stop wireframing for days. Google just turned a text prompt into production-ready frontend code.

Stitch by Google is an AI design canvas that converts natural language into real UI and code.

→ Sketch, screenshot, or text becomes a working UI
→ A built-in agent writes PRDs and critiques your build
→ DESIGN.md lets you port design rules across tools
→ Voice mode: talk to the canvas, it updates live

It runs on Gemini under the hood, reading multimodal inputs as creative seeds, then generating clean HTML/CSS with a context-aware agent tracking every decision.

The design-to-dev handoff just collapsed into one tool.
https://t.co/G0KPjbiE9Z

items.content:
Anthropic just went global with its developer conference.

Code with Claude is returning, now across San Francisco, London, and Tokyo.

→ Full-day hands-on workshops
→ Live product demos
→ 1:1 office hours with the actual builders
→ Free livestream open to anyone

The first edition was one city. Now it's three continents.

The API, CLI tools, and MCP are the core focus. You can now sit across from the team and show them exactly where your workflow breaks.

@deebeeeff 1) we're still working on the ui, please give us feedback!

there's dozens of new systems here, we appreciate your help and thoughts to look at them all <3

items.title: MolmoPoint is also easier to train—with just 8K examples it outperforms coordinate-based models by ~...
items.content:
MolmoPoint is also easier to train—with just 8K examples it outperforms coordinate-based models by ~20 F1 points & reaches peak performance faster during full pretraining.

These grounding gains don't come at a cost. Question-answering, captioning, & other tasks all stay on par.

items.title:
You would never remodel your kitchen without a plan.
You would never shoot a movie without a script....
items.content:
You would never remodel your kitchen without a plan.
You would never shoot a movie without a script.
You would never fly a plane without a map.

Do not let agents change your code without first writing a plan.

I'm glad more and more tools are focusing on planning before you write any code.

We don't need more code. We need better code.

items.title:
Google just updated its UI creation tool Stitch with what it's calling "vibe design."

A few standou...
items.content:
Google just updated its UI creation tool Stitch with what it's calling "vibe design."

A few standout features: 

- Voice control that lets you speak directly to your design canvas and get real-time critiques

- Instant prototyping that turns static screens into interactive prototypes in seconds + auto-generating logical next screens with an infinite canvas

- An agent that reasons across full project history, with DESIGN.md files for portable design systems

It's kind of ridiculous at this point, but I'm u...
items.content:
I run every one of my businesses off spreadsheets.

It's kind of ridiculous at this point, but I'm used to doing this, and it works for me.

I have a bunch of Claude Code skills to manage everything, but I'd love to have an agent that 100% specializes in Excel.

This is very promising!

MolmoPoint works in three steps using special grounding tokens:

1️⃣ Pick a rough region of the imag...
items.content:
MolmoPoint works in three steps using special grounding tokens:

1️⃣ Pick a rough region of the image/video that contains the target
2️⃣ Zoom in to a smaller area using finer visual features
3️⃣ Pinpoint the exact pixel-level location within that area
items.author: @allen_ai

3️⃣ Pinpoint the exact pixel-level location within that area

items.title:
MolmoPoint takes a different approach.

Instead of writing coordinates, the model points by selectin...
items.content:
MolmoPoint takes a different approach.

Instead of writing coordinates, the model points by selecting from the visual tokens it's already looking at—like the difference between reading out "position 347, 582" and tapping directly on a touchscreen.

items.title:
If you are building agentic apps and want funding:

The TinyFish Accelerator is looking for founders...
items.content:
If you are building agentic apps and want funding:

The TinyFish Accelerator is looking for founders who want to build agents that navigate real web interfaces and execute real transactions.

It's a 9-week accelerator program with a $2M seed pool.

Here is what you get:

• Free TinyFish API keys
• Engineering support and product feedback from the team
• Free credits + direct engineering access from partner companies
• Weekly business mentorship sessions with founders and leaders
• Off-the-record community meetups
• Pitch directly to Robin Vasan (Mango Capital)

To apply, you need a working app + record a demo, and publish it on X.

So far, 18 founders have joined! I'm posting below 3 of my favorite applications.

Apply → https://t.co/xON0UCoSTl

Applications are open until the end of March.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama promotes TinyFish Accelerator - a 9-week program with $2M seed pool for founders building agents that navigate real web interfaces and execute transactions.

items.title:
The biggest shift is zero-shot voice cloning.

→ No training phase
→ No dataset collection
→ No fine...
items.content:
The biggest shift is zero-shot voice cloning.

→ No training phase
→ No dataset collection
→ No fine-tuning

Just a few seconds of audio and the model replicates tone, cadence, and identity instantly.

This removes the biggest bottleneck in voice AI adoption.

Voice is becoming as programmable as text.

items.title: Most VLMs point by generating text coordinates—essentially dictating numbers. It works, but it waste...
items.content: Most VLMs point by generating text coordinates—essentially dictating numbers. It works, but it wastes tokens, breaks at high resolutions, and forces models to learn an abstract numbering system that has nothing to do with how they actually see.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Describes the problem MolmoPoint solves: coordinate-based pointing wastes tokens and forces abstract numbering
items.reasoning: Problem framing for the thread
items.summary_html: Describes the problem MolmoPoint solves: coordinate-based pointing wastes tokens and forces abstract numbering
items.content_html: Most VLMs point by generating text coordinates—essentially dictating numbers. It works, but it wastes tokens, breaks at high resolutions, and forces models to learn an abstract numbering system that has nothing to do with how they actually see.

AI agents are powerful.

But here’s the uncomfortable truth:

Anyone can build an AI agent.
Very few can deploy one safely at enterprise scale.

I’m digging into this with nvidia, and it’s about to matter to every enterprise team.

Let’s talk about why…

items.title:
The real shift happening right now is this:

Enterprises are moving from prototypes to real-world ag...
items.content:
The real shift happening right now is this:

Enterprises are moving from prototypes to real-world agentic systems.

That requires:

→ Open, trusted foundations
→ End-to-end observability
→ Tracing that shows how the agent arrived at a decision

Because “it worked in the demo” is not a deployment strategy.

items.title:
Most teams think the hard part is “making the agent work.”

In enterprise, the hard part is differen...
items.content:
Most teams think the hard part is “making the agent work.”

In enterprise, the hard part is different:

→ Can you trust it?
→ Can you explain it?
→ Can you control it?
→ Can you prove it’s behaving correctly?

If you can’t, you don’t have an AI agent.
You have a liability.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

In enterprise, the hard part is different:

→ Can you trust it?

→ Can you explain it?

→ Can you control it?

→ Can you prove it’s behaving correctly?

If you can’t, you don’t have an AI agent.

You have a liability.

items.title:
But realism isn’t just about sounding right.

It’s about feeling right.

New systems introduce:

→ E...
items.content:
But realism isn’t just about sounding right.

It’s about feeling right.

New systems introduce:

→ Expressive control, calm, neutral, dramatic delivery
→ Paralinguistic prompts like [laugh], [sigh], [gasp]
→ Real-time performance for assistants, games, interactive media

We’re moving from synthetic voices to performative AI.

That’s a very different category.

But realism isn’t just about sounding right.

It’s about feeling right.

New systems introduce:

→ Expressive control, calm, neutral, dramatic delivery

→ Paralinguistic prompts like [laugh], [sigh], [gasp]

→ Real-time performance for assistants, games, interactive media

We’re moving from synthetic voices to performative AI.

That’s a very different category.

MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning!  @MIT

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month:

items.title:
Stop wiring communication APIs by hand. Lovable just made Twilio a one-prompt integration.

Lovable,...
items.content:
Stop wiring communication APIs by hand. Lovable just made Twilio a one-prompt integration.

Lovable, the AI app builder, now lets you add phone-based features without writing backend code.

→ SMS and WhatsApp out of the box
→ 2FA login flows, no setup headache
→ AI voice agents, from a single prompt

Credentials stay secure. API keys are handled through Lovable Cloud, stored as secrets and accessed only through Edge Functions.

Before this, you had to manually configure SDKs, write service files, and manage auth yourself. Now it's a connector you just turn on.

Shared connectors act like plug-ins your app calls whenever it needs to perform an action. Twilio is now one of them.

Any app you build can now have real-time communication built in, on day one.
https://t.co/3AKbmIRiHG

@michellejmayes Code Review is our opinionated take that is best at finding bugs. 

The github actio...
items.content:
@michellejmayes Code Review is our opinionated take that is best at finding bugs. 

The github action is something you can customize more to specifically what you want (e.g. maybe you want it to check if any skills should be updated as part of this change). Generally takes less compute.
items.author: @trq212
items.summary: Explanation of Code Review vs GitHub Action - Code Review is opinionated and best at finding bugs, while GitHub Action is more customizable and uses less compute.

items.content_html:
@michellejmayes Code Review is our opinionated take that is best at finding bugs.

The github action is something you can customize more to specifically what you want (e.g. maybe you want it to check if any skills should be updated as part of this change). Generally takes less compute.

items.title: 💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]:...
items.content:
💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]: https://t.co/Z32D8jOzxC v/ @PacktDataML

𝓒𝓸𝓷𝓽𝓮𝓷𝓽𝓼:
🔹Common Conventions & API Elements of Scikit-Learn
🔹Pre-Model Workflow and Data Preprocessing
🔹Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
🔹Build Models with Distance Metrics & Nearest Neighbors
🔹Linear Models and Regularization
🔹Advanced Logistic Regression and Extensions
🔹Support Vector Machines and Kernel Methods
🔹Tree-Based Algorithms and Ensemble Methods
🔹Text Processing and Multiclass Classification
🔹Clustering Techniques
🔹Novelty and Outlier Detection
🔹Cross-Validation and Model Evaluation Techniques
🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.

items.title:
@georgwtf Only 11% (or $99B) of that are companies founded after 1950

89% ($879B) of that is spin-o...
items.content:
@georgwtf Only 11% (or $99B) of that are companies founded after 1950

89% ($879B) of that is spin-offs or mergers from companies founded before 1950

Even then you're talking about $99B vs US market size of $22.3T (or 225x bigger)

If you include the entire European public tech market cap it's $0.4T

So EU's tech market cap is 60x smaller but it has 2x bigger population

items.summary: Levelsio shares detailed analysis of EU vs US tech market caps, showing EU tech is 60x smaller with 2x the population

items.title:
None of Philips electronics products are owned or made by Philips

Only their medical devices still ...
items.content:
None of Philips electronics products are owned or made by Philips

Only their medical devices still are

They sold literally everything (even their lights division)

Now they license the Philips logo to whoever wants it
 
Yes you too can make anything and pay them some money to stick the Philips logo on top of it

It all means nothing!

items.title: This is ridiculous, @TangerineBank. You send me an email asking to finish the application that I aba...
items.content:
This is ridiculous, @TangerineBank. You send me an email asking to finish the application that I abandoned earlier, but you send your email from a noreply address.

My answer to emails from noreply addresses is GFYS.

For your culture: A noreply email is read by a person as follows: "Hey! We need something from you, but if you need something back from us, you can go fuck yourself. Have a nice day!"
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov complains about receiving emails from noreply addresses
items.reasoning: Off-topic rant about banking, not AI-related
items.summary_html: Burkov complains about receiving emails from noreply addresses
items.content_html:
This is ridiculous, @TangerineBank. You send me an email asking to finish the application that I abandoned earlier, but you send your email from a noreply address.

My answer to emails from noreply addresses is GFYS.

For your culture: A noreply email is read by a person as follows: "Hey! We need something from you, but if you need something back from us, you can go fuck yourself. Have a nice day!"
